questions.questionText: What city served as the capital of the United States from 1790 to 1800?
questions.tidbits: The Boston Tea Party took place on December 16, 1773, when colonists dumped 342 chests of East India Company tea into Boston Harbor.

questions.questionText: Which river forms the eastern boundary between New York and New Jersey?
questions.tidbits: The Proclamation of 1763 aimed to avoid conflicts between colonists and Native Americans, but many colonists ignored it.

questions.questionText: What body of water did George Washington famously cross on the night of December 25–26, 1776?
questions.tidbits: The Intolerable Acts were passed in response to the Boston Tea Party and included closing Boston Harbor until the tea was paid for.

questions.questionText: The Battle of Bunker Hill was actually fought on which nearby hill?
questions.tidbits: Patrick Henry delivered his famous "Give me liberty, or give me death!" speech in March 1775 at the Second Virginia Convention.

questions.questionText: Which colonial port city was blockaded by the British in 1774 as punishment for the Tea Party?
questions.tidbits: The Battle of Bunker Hill (actually fought on Breed's Hill) showed that colonial militia could stand up to professional British soldiers.

questions.questionText: What Virginia city served as the colonial capital before Richmond?
The Siege of Yorktown — the final major battle of the Revolution — took place in which colony?
Fort Ticonderoga, captured by Ethan Allen in 1775, sits at the southern end of which lake?
The Proclamation of 1763 forbade colonial settlement west of which mountain range?
The Treaty of Paris (1783) set the western boundary of the United States at which river?
Who wrote the influential 1776 pamphlet 'Common Sense'?
questions.tidbits: Thomas Paine's Common Sense sold about 500,000 copies in its first year — an extraordinary number for a population of just 2.5 million.

questions.questionText: Benjamin Franklin published which famous almanac annually from 1732 to 1758?
questions.tidbits: The Declaration was primarily written by Thomas Jefferson over about 17 days in June 1776.

questions.questionText: What popular colonial tavern in Boston was a gathering place for Sons of Liberty?
questions.tidbits: Most signers of the Declaration were lawyers, merchants, or planters — relatively wealthy and educated men.

questions.questionText: Which Boston newspaper, founded in 1690, was the first newspaper published in the American colonies?
questions.answerText: Publick Occurrences Both Foreign and Domestick
questions.tidbits: Washington's crossing of the Delaware on Christmas night 1776 led to surprise victories at Trenton and Princeton.

questions.questionText: John Peter Zenger's 1735 trial established an early precedent for which freedom in America?
questions.tidbits: Valley Forge (1777–78) was a turning point where Friedrich von Steuben drilled the Continental Army into a more professional fighting force.

questions.questionText: The Federalist Papers were published under what collective pen name?
Which 1765 law sparked widespread colonial protest because it taxed legal documents and printed materials?
Which song, originally a British tune used to mock colonists, was adopted by American patriots as their own marching song?
Thomas Paine's follow-up pamphlet series published during the Revolutionary War was titled what?
Which colonial-era newspaper, founded by Benjamin Franklin's brother James in 1721, openly criticized the colonial government?
In what year was the Declaration of Independence adopted? A) 1774, B) 1775, C) 1776, D) 1778
questions.tidbits: Benjamin Franklin was 70 years old when he signed the Declaration of Independence, making him the oldest signer.

questions.questionText: Who was the primary author of the Declaration of Independence?
questions.tidbits: The Constitutional Convention met in secret — windows were nailed shut in summer heat to keep deliberations confidential.

questions.questionText: George Washington was sworn in as the first President of the United States in what year? A) 1787, B) 1788, C) 1789, D) 1790
questions.tidbits: Alexander Hamilton wrote 51 of the 85 Federalist Papers, making it his most prolific literary project.

questions.questionText: The Constitutional Convention was held in which city in 1787?
questions.tidbits: The Bill of Rights was ratified on December 15, 1791, adding the first 10 amendments to the Constitution.

questions.questionText: What event on December 16, 1773 involved colonists dumping 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor?
questions.tidbits: The term "Midnight Judges" refers to appointments John Adams made the night before Jefferson's inauguration.

questions.questionText: Which battle on April 19, 1775 is known as 'the shot heard round the world'?
The Articles of Confederation were replaced by the U.S. Constitution, which was ratified in what year? A) 1785, B) 1786, C) 1787, D) 1788
The Bill of Rights — the first ten amendments — was ratified in what year? A) 1789, B) 1790, C) 1791, D) 1793
How many of the original thirteen colonies had to ratify the Constitution to make it effective? A) Seven, B) Eight, C) Nine, D) Ten
The XYZ Affair of 1797–1798 nearly brought the United States into war with which country?
Which artist painted the famous portrait of George Washington that appears on the one-dollar bill?
questions.tidbits: Gilbert Stuart's unfinished portrait of Washington (the "Athenaeum portrait") became the most reproduced portrait in American history and appears on the dollar bill.

questions.questionText: Paul Revere created a famous 1770 engraving depicting which violent confrontation between British soldiers and colonists?
questions.tidbits: Phillis Wheatley was the first African American and one of the first American women to publish a book of poetry.

questions.questionText: What bird was chosen as the national symbol on the Great Seal of the United States, adopted in 1782?
questions.tidbits: The Liberty Bell cracked sometime in the 19th century; it had already been recast twice since its arrival from England in 1752.

questions.questionText: Betsy Ross is traditionally credited with sewing the first version of what object in 1776?
questions.tidbits: The Great Seal of the United States was approved by Congress on June 20, 1782, and took six years and three committees to finalize.

questions.questionText: The reverse of the Great Seal features an unfinished pyramid with how many levels?
questions.tidbits: "Yankee Doodle" was originally a British song meant to mock American colonists; colonists adopted it defiantly as their own.

questions.questionText: John Trumbull's painting 'Declaration of Independence' depicts the presentation of the draft to what body?
Which female allegorical figure, often depicted with a torch and shield, symbolized American liberty in the 1700s?
Charles Willson Peale founded the first major American art museum in Philadelphia in 1786 — what was it called?
The Latin phrase 'E Pluribus Unum' on the Great Seal means what in English?
Paul Revere was by profession primarily a craftsman in which material, before becoming famous as a patriot?
Benjamin Franklin's famous kite experiment in 1752 demonstrated that lightning was a form of what?
questions.tidbits: The triangular trade linked Europe, Africa, and the Americas; enslaved Africans were traded for sugar, tobacco, and rum.

questions.questionText: Franklin invented a rod placed on buildings to protect them from lightning strikes — what is it called?
questions.tidbits: Continental currency depreciated so severely that the phrase "not worth a Continental" became a common expression.

questions.questionText: Benjamin Franklin invented eyeglasses with two lenses of different focal lengths — what are they called?
questions.tidbits: France's entry into the Revolutionary War in 1778 transformed it into a global conflict stretching to India and the Caribbean.

questions.questionText: The printing press used in colonial America was based on technology invented by whom in the 1440s?
questions.tidbits: The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 is considered one of the most significant acts of the Continental Congress — it established a framework for admitting new states.

questions.questionText: David Bushnell built the 'Turtle' in 1775 — the first military submarine. Which revolutionary war force did it serve?
questions.tidbits: Jay's Treaty was deeply unpopular; mobs burned John Jay in effigy and Alexander Hamilton was stoned while trying to defend it.

questions.questionText: Benjamin Franklin invented a heat-efficient fireplace insert in 1741 — what is it commonly called?
If a colonial farmer plants corn in rows of 8 with 12 rows total, how many corn plants did he plant?
The dominant three-masted square-rigged sailing warship of the Revolutionary War era was commonly called what?
John Fitch demonstrated the first successful American steamboat on the Delaware River in what year? A) 1783, B) 1785, C) 1787, D) 1790
Oliver Evans designed an automated flour mill in the 1780s that used what system to move grain without manual labor?
What was the most popular spectator sport among wealthy colonial Americans in the 1700s, especially in Virginia?
questions.tidbits: George Rogers Clark's 1779 capture of Fort Sackville in present-day Indiana helped secure the Old Northwest for the United States.

questions.questionText: George Washington was known to enjoy which fox-related outdoor sport on his estate at Mount Vernon?
questions.tidbits: The Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 was the first major test of federal authority; Washington personally led troops — the only sitting president to command in the field.

questions.questionText: What popular colonial yard game involved throwing a horseshoe at a stake in the ground?
questions.tidbits: The XYZ Affair referred to the three unnamed French agents who demanded bribes before diplomatic talks, sparking public outrage in the U.S.

questions.questionText: Colonial armed citizen soldiers required to drill and respond at a minute's notice were called what?
questions.tidbits: The Louisiana Purchase doubled the size of the United States for about 3 cents per acre.

questions.questionText: What early American bowling-like game played with nine pins was popular in colonial taverns?
questions.tidbits: The Lewis and Clark Expedition traveled roughly 8,000 miles round-trip from 1804 to 1806.

questions.questionText: Gander pulling was a rough colonial game in which contestants tried to pull off the head of what animal while riding on horseback?
Which card game, imported from Europe, was the most popular gambling card game in colonial American taverns?
At colonial fairs and musters, what competitive shooting event had participants firing rifles at a target for prizes?
The earliest known American horse race course was established in 1665 on Long Island — what was it called?
What colonial bat-and-ball game, an ancestor of modern baseball, involved hitting a ball and running to bases or posts?
